title: WHEN TO USE IT, WHEN TO SKIP IT

captions.text: every turn re-reads a thread that every previous turn made longer

lines.text: So when does this earn its cost? Four triggers. The question is a genuine tradeoff. The cost of being wrong is high. The domain invites confident hallucination. Or you need the reasoning, and the open questions, more than the verdict itself.

And when should you skip it? Latency-sensitive paths, obviously. A multi-round debate is a dozen sequential LLM calls, because that’s literally what it is. Simple factual queries gain nothing. And watch the cost curve: every turn re-reads a thread that every previous turn made longer. That grows closer to quadratically than linearly. Fine for ten architecture decisions a week. Ruinous for a million support tickets a day.

One more pitfall, and it’s the big one. Echo chambers. Same base model, same blind spots. If the model believes something wrong, the Advocate asserts it and the Critic lets it stand. Role prompts catch unsupported reasoning. They cannot conjure knowledge the model lacks. Mitigations, in increasing order of effectiveness: make the critic concretely adversarial, use a different model family for the critic, and ground the debate in retrieved documents so evidence settles disagreements, instead of whoever sounds most confident.
